eli3: Standard deviation is the square root of variance. It answers: on average, how far do individual data points sit from the mean? Taking the square root brings the unit back to the original scale — so if data is in dollars, standard deviation is also in dollars.
For a normal distribution, about 68% of data falls within ±1σ of the mean, 95% within ±2σ, and 99.7% within ±3σ. This '68-95-99.7 rule' is one of the most-used rules of thumb in statistics and ML.

Standard deviation is the natural measure of spread for normal-like data. It also appears in the formula for z-scores: z = (x−μ)/σ, which tells you how many standard deviations a value is from the mean — the basis for standardisation, z-tests, and normal tables.

whenWhy.title: Measuring and comparing spread across variables
whenWhy.body: Standard deviation expresses spread in the same units as the data (unlike variance, which is in squared units). It enables direct comparison: 'these test scores have σ=10, those have σ=5' — the first group is twice as spread out.

whenWhy.title: Standardisation / z-score normalisation
whenWhy.body: z = (x−μ)/σ transforms data to mean 0, std 1. This is critical before algorithms that are sensitive to feature scale (KNN, SVM, PCA, neural networks). Without standardisation, features with large scales dominate distance-based and gradient-based computations.

whenWhy.title: Confidence intervals and hypothesis tests
whenWhy.body: The standard error of the mean (σ/√n) is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of x̄. It quantifies how precisely the sample mean estimates the population mean. A 95% CI for μ is approximately x̄ ± 1.96σ/√n. All z-tests and t-tests normalise by the standard error.

whenWhy.title: Volatility in finance
whenWhy.body: Annualised volatility of a financial asset is the annualised standard deviation of log-returns: σ_annual = σ_daily × √252. It is the core input to Black-Scholes option pricing and risk measures like VaR. Higher σ means more uncertain outcomes.

deepDiveIntro: The sample standard deviation is the square root of the sample variance, computed with n-minus-one in the denominator to make the variance unbiased. Taking the square root introduces a small downward bias in the standard deviation itself because the square root is a concave function, but this correction is negligible for samples larger than about thirty and is rarely applied in practice.

For normally distributed data, the standard deviation fully characterizes the spread. The 68-95-99.7 rule follows directly from the areas under the normal curve within one, two, and three standard deviations of the mean respectively.

deepDive.heading: Definition and Relationship to Variance
deepDive.body: Standard deviation is the square root version of variance, which brings spread back onto the original units of the data. That makes it much easier to interpret in practice than variance itself. The sample version is based on the unbiased sample variance, although the square root step introduces a small bias that is usually ignored in routine work.

deepDive.heading: Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7)
deepDive.body: For normal data, standard deviation has a direct geometric interpretation through the familiar sixty-eight, ninety-five, and ninety-nine-point-seven rule. That rule does not hold exactly for arbitrary distributions, but it explains why standard deviation is so intuitive in bell-shaped settings. Outside the normal case, broader inequalities give weaker but more general guarantees.

deepDive.heading: Standard Error of the Mean
deepDive.body: The standard error is the standard deviation of an estimator rather than of the raw data. For the sample mean, it shrinks with the square root of sample size, which explains why gaining a little extra precision often requires much more data. This is the scale that drives confidence intervals and many hypothesis tests.

deepDive.heading: Z-score Standardisation
deepDive.body: Standard deviation is also the scale used to standardize data. Converting values to z-scores puts features onto a common unitless scale, which is why standardization is so important for principal component analysis, regularized models, and many gradient-based learning algorithms. It turns raw differences into comparable distances from the center.

takeaways: s = √[Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1)]; same units as x — more interpretable than variance. [1]
s² is unbiased for σ², but s is slightly biased for σ (Jensen's inequality — minor for n>30). [1]
68-95-99.7 rule: for N(μ,σ²), ≈68/95/99.7% of data falls within 1/2/3σ of mean. [1]
Standard error SE = σ/√n: standard deviation of the sampling distribution of x̄. [1]
Z-score z=(x−μ)/σ standardises to N(0,1) — basis of z-tests and feature scaling. [1][2]
Chebyshev: P(|X−μ| ≥ kσ) ≤ 1/k² — distribution-free bound using only σ. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why is the coefficient of variation (CV) sometimes preferred over standard deviation?
edgeCases.a: CV = σ/μ × 100% is a dimensionless measure of relative variability. It allows comparing spread across variables with different scales or units: a σ=5 may be large if μ=10 (CV=50%) but small if μ=1000 (CV=0.5%). CV is meaningful only for ratio-scale data with a positive mean (not for data that can be zero or negative). [1]

edgeCases.q: When should you use MAD instead of standard deviation?
edgeCases.a: MAD = median(|xᵢ−median(x)|) is a robust alternative to σ. It has breakdown point 0.5 (like the median) vs σ's breakdown point of 0. Robust σ̂ = 1.4826 × MAD is a consistent estimator of σ for normal data. Use MAD when data has heavy tails, or when robustness to outliers is more important than efficiency under normality. [2]

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 2
